source_paper_title: Tight Bounds for Secretary Matching in General Graphs

title: Designing polynomial-time 1/4-competitive secretary matching algorithm under edge arrivals
background_latex:
Consider an undirected weighted graph \(G=(V,E)\) with \(|E|=m\). Each edge \(e\in E\) has an (adversarial) nonnegative weight \(w_e\in\mathbb{R}\) that is unknown until the edge arrives online. In the edge-arrival secretary model, the edges \(e_1,\dots,e_m\) arrive in a uniformly random order. When edge \(e_t=uv\) arrives at time \(t\), its weight \(w_{e_t}\) is revealed, and the algorithm must immediately and irrevocably decide whether to add \(e_t\) to its matching \(\mu\), subject to feasibility (both endpoints \(u,v\) are currently unmatched).

Let \(\mu^*(E)\) denote a maximum-weight matching in \(G\) (with respect to \(w\)), and let \(\mathrm{OPT}=w(\mu^*(E))=\sum_{e\in\mu^*(E)} w_e\). An online algorithm \(\mathrm{ALG}\) outputs a random matching \(\mu\) and achieves competitive ratio \(\alpha\in[0,1]\) if for every weighted graph instance \((G,w)\),
\[\mathbb{E}[w(\mu)] \ge \alpha\, \mathrm{OPT},\]
where the expectation is over the random arrival order and the algorithm’s internal randomness.

The paper gives an information-theoretic online algorithm for edge arrivals that achieves \(\alpha=1/4\), but whose implementation appears to require estimating certain conditional availability probabilities that the authors do not know how to compute in polynomial time.
problem_definition_latex:
Find a polynomial-time online algorithm \(\mathrm{ALG}\) for the edge-arrival secretary matching model such that for every weighted graph \((G=(V,E),w)\) with \(|E|=m\),
\[\mathbb{E}[w(\mu)] \ge \tfrac{1}{4}\, w(\mu^*(E)),\]
where \(\mu\) is the matching output by \(\mathrm{ALG}\), and the expectation is over the uniformly random arrival order of edges and the randomness of \(\mathrm{ALG}\).
latex:
Consider an undirected weighted graph \(G=(V,E)\) with \(|E|=m\). Each edge \(e\in E\) has an (adversarial) nonnegative weight \(w_e\in\mathbb{R}\) that is unknown until the edge arrives online. When edge \(e_t=uv\) arrives at time \(t\), its weight \(w_{e_t}\) is revealed, and the algorithm must immediately and irrevocably decide whether to add \(e_t\) to its matching \(\mu\), subject to feasibility (both endpoints \(u,v\) are currently unmatched).

Let \(\mu^*(E)\) denote a maximum-weight matching in \(G\) (with respect to \(w\)), and let \(\mathrm{OPT}=w(\mu^*(E))=\sum_{e\in\mu^*(E)} w_e\). An online algorithm \(\mathrm{ALG}\) outputs a random matching \(\mu\) and achieves competitive ratio \(\alpha\in[0,1]\) if for every weighted graph instance \((G,w)\),

\[\mathbb{E}[w(\mu)] \ge \tfrac{1}{4}\, w(\mu^*(E)),\]

location_in_paper: Page 6, Section 1.1 (Edge arrival techniques discussion); and reiterated Page 23, Remark after Theorem 5.2.
open_problem_quote:
Interestingly, despite the conceptual simplicity of Algorithm 2, it is unclear how to implement it in polynomial time. Thus, our result for the edge arrival model is information theoretic. Obtaining a poly-time algorithm with this ratio remains as an interesting problem.

Thus, our result in Theorem 5.2 can be seen as an information-theoretic result. It remains an interesting problem whether there is a poly-time online algorithm that matches this bound of 1{4.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The open question is existential (design an efficient algorithm) rather than a universal claim, so counterexample search is not directly applicable. One could search for hard instances for candidate algorithms, but that would not resolve existence of some other polynomial-time method achieving 1/4.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate online policies (e.g., threshold rules or sampling-based selection probabilities) can be parameterized and evaluated via simulation on random-order edge streams with computable payoff versus offline optimum on generated graphs. However, the objective is worst-case over all weighted graphs, so simulation provides only a heuristic fitness proxy and may miss adversarial constructions.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper of Ezra, Feldman, Gravin, and Tang (2020) establishes a sharp separation between what is achievable information-theoretically and what is currently known to be efficiently implementable for secretary matching under edge arrivals in general graphs. Their Algorithm 2 achieves the optimal-looking constant \(1/4\) by controlling, for each time \(t\), the conditional acceptance probability of the last-arriving edge \(e_t\) given that \(e_t\) lies in the current maximum-weight matching \(\mu_t^*\). The acceptance probability is \(\alpha_t/x_t\), where \(x_t\) is the conditional probability that both endpoints of \(e_t\) are still available. The analysis uses a union-bound style availability lower bound \(x_t \ge 1-2\sum_{i<t} \alpha_i/i\) and chooses \(\alpha_t\) via the recurrence \(\alpha_t = 1-2\sum_{i<t} \alpha_i/i\) after an exploration phase, yielding \(\mathbb{E}[w(\mu)] \ge (1/4)\,\mathrm{OPT}\). The bottleneck is computational: evaluating \(x_t\) appears to require reasoning about the algorithm’s behavior on many possible subsets \(E_{t-1}\), which the authors do not know how to do in polynomial time.

Prior polynomial-time guarantees for edge-arrival secretary matching in general graphs are weaker, notably the \(1/(2e)\) bound of Kesselheim, Radke, Tönnis, and Vöcking (2013), obtained via reductions to related arrival models. Related prophet-inequality work (e.g., Gravin and Wang 2019 for bipartite matching and Ezra et al. 2020 for general graphs) develops OCRS-inspired, often non-adaptive or threshold-based policies where availability probabilities can be bounded without instance-dependent conditional computations; these techniques motivate the information-theoretic secretary policy but do not directly yield a \(1/4\) polynomial-time secretary algorithm.

As of the evidence available here (no forward-citation analyses provided), the specific open problem remains unresolved: there is no known polynomial-time online algorithm for edge-arrival secretary matching in general graphs that achieves the \(1/4\) competitive ratio of the information-theoretic Algorithm 2. Promising directions suggested by the surrounding literature include finding a surrogate for \(x_t\) that is efficiently estimable online (e.g., via pessimistic bounds, non-adaptive acceptance schedules, or coupling to OCRS-style contention resolution), or designing a different algorithm whose acceptance probabilities depend only on time \(t\) and local information yet can be analyzed to match \(1/4\).

sources.title: Secretary Matching with General Arrivals
sources.theorem_or_result: Gives an information-theoretic 1/4-competitive algorithm for weighted matching in general graphs under uniformly random edge arrivals (Algorithm 2), but notes it is unclear how to implement in polynomial time because it requires computing conditional availability probabilities \(x_t\).
sources.connection: This is the source paper that poses the open problem: derandomize/implement the 1/4-competitive edge-arrival policy in polynomial time (or design a different poly-time 1/4-competitive algorithm).

sources.title: An optimal online algorithm for weighted bipartite matching and extensions to combinatorial auctions
sources.theorem_or_result: Establishes a \(1/(2e)\)-competitive algorithm for secretary matching in general graphs under edge arrivals via a reduction to vertex arrivals in hypergraphs; also proves tight \(1/e\) for 1-sided bipartite vertex-arrival secretary matching.
sources.connection: Provides the prior best polynomial-time guarantee for edge-arrival secretary matching (\(1/(2e)\)), which the open problem seeks to improve to \(1/4\) with a poly-time algorithm.

sources.title: Prophet inequality for bipartite matching: Merits of being simple and non adaptive
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies edge-arrival prophet inequalities for bipartite matching; gives a simple non-adaptive \(1/3\)-competitive algorithm and an upper bound of \(4/9\).
sources.connection: Conceptually related: prophet-inequality techniques (e.g., OCRS-style control of acceptance probabilities) inspire the information-theoretic edge-arrival secretary algorithm; may suggest implementable threshold policies.

sources.title: Online stochastic max-weight matching: Prophet inequality for vertex and edge arrival models
sources.theorem_or_result: Gives prophet-inequality algorithms for max-weight matching under vertex and edge arrivals in general graphs, including an OCRS-style approach achieving competitive ratio about 0.337 for edge arrivals.
sources.connection: Provides the prophet-inequality counterpart and technical toolkit (OCRS, availability via union bounds) that the secretary edge-arrival algorithm adapts; may offer ideas for efficient implementation via non-adaptive policies.

sources.title: Algorithms for secretary problems on graphs and hypergraphs
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces secretary problems on graphs/hypergraphs; gives early constant-factor algorithms for matching variants and hypergraph secretary matching with degree/size dependence.
sources.connection: Foundational for secretary matching under various arrival models; the edge-arrival model in the open problem is a central case in this line.

sources.title: Combinatorial secretary problems with ordinal information
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies secretary problems when only ordinal comparisons are observed; gives constant-competitive algorithms for several constraints including matching under vertex arrivals.
sources.connection: Not directly solving edge-arrival poly-time 1/4, but relevant as it shows implementable greedy/ordinal surrogates and highlights gaps between information-theoretic and efficiently implementable policies.
